original_abstract:
This study investigates the formation of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) from the ozonolysis and hydroxyl radical (OH)-initiated oxidation of $\alpha$-pinene under high nitric oxide (NO) conditions, exploring the influences of relative humidity (RH) and aerosol acidity. Experiments conducted in the University of North Carolina dual outdoor smog chamber revealed that $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis produced significantly more SOA mass than OH-initiated oxidation under high-NO conditions, though changes in RH did not significantly alter the total mass. Interestingly, while O$_3$-initiated oxidation on pure ammonium sulfate (AS) seeds led to robust nucleation, seeding with organic-coated AS particles suppressed this process.

Detailed chemical characterization using UPLC/ESI-HR-Q-TOFMS identified eight carboxylic acids and four high-molecular-weight dimers, accounting for 8–12% of the total SOA mass. OH-initiated oxidation in the presence of NO$_x$ favored the formation of highly oxidized species such as 3-methyl-1,2,3-butanetricarboxylic acid (MBTCA) and diaterpenylic acid acetate (DTAA). In contrast, dimers were only detected in SOA generated via ozonolysis in the absence of NO$_x$. The concentration of these dimers doubled at higher RH (50–90%) compared to lower RH (30–50%), a trend that correlated with enhanced new particle formation. Notably, aerosol acidity and total SOA mass yield did not significantly influence the abundance of these species.

Our findings suggest that these high-molecular-weight dimers are formed via gas-phase reactions involving stabilized Criegee Intermediates (sCIs). Due to their low volatility and high polarity, these dimers likely drive homogeneous nucleation, increasing particle number concentrations. Given that three identified dimers (MW 358, 344, and 368) are consistently found in both laboratory and ambient samples, we propose their use as molecular tracers for $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis. These results highlight the complex interplay between anthropogenic emissions (NO$_x$) and natural biogenic precursors in governsing aerosol formation and evolution.
humanized_abstract:
Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formed from the ozonolysis and hydroxyl radical (OH)-initiated oxidation of α-pinene in high nitric oxide (NO) levels was studied in different relative humidity (RH) and aerosol acidity conditions in the University of North Carolina dual outdoor smog chamber. In general, the ozonolysis pathway generated about 4 times more SOA mass than that produced from the OH-initiated oxidation pathway, while little variation in total mass yield was found with RH. In addition, while O3-initiated oxidation on pure ammonium sulfate (AS) seeds facilitated strong nucleation, seeding with organic-coated AS particles significantly suppressed this nucleation.

We here show for the first time, using UPLC/ESI-HR-Q-TOFMS analysis, the composition of specific carboxylic acids and dimers in SOA, that they constituted 8–12% of the total mass, consisting of 8 carboxylic acids ranging from 102 to 256 Da and 4 high molecular weight dimers ranging from 544 to 694 Da. Generally, the majority of oxidation products formed through OH-initiated oxidation in the presence of NO$_x$ were highly oxidised as for example 3-methyl-1,2,3-butanetricarboxylic acid (MBTCA) and diaterpenylic acid acetate (DTAA). Dimers were only found in ozonolysis SOA under NO-free conditions. The amount of dimers doubled at higher RH (50–90%RH) than at lower RH (30–50%RH) as did the amount of freshly formed nucleated particles. The acidities of the aerosol as well as the total SOA mass yields showed only very minor effects.

The high-molecular-weight dimers were observed to be formed through gas-phase reactions of stabilized Criegee Intermediates (sCIs). Due to their low-volatility and high-polarity, the dimers were attributed to cause homogeneous nucleation and, consequently, the increase in particle number concentration. It was found that the three dimers having molecular weights (MW) of 358, 344, and 368, were consistently observed in both laboratory and ambient atmosphere, and therefore could serve as potential molecular tracers of $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis. This study demonstrates the importance of interactions between anthropogenic and biogenic emissions in determining aerosol formation and evolution processes.

document.output:
Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formed from the ozonolysis and hydroxyl radical (OH)-initiated oxidation of α-pinene in high nitric oxide (NO) levels was studied in different relative humidity (RH) and aerosol acidity conditions in the University of North Carolina dual outdoor smog chamber. It was found that the three dimers having molecular weights (MW) of 358, 344, and 368, were consistently observed in both laboratory and ambient atmosphere, and therefore could serve as potential molecular tracers of $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis.
